9.reason: [This prints the permutation as a single integer, which fails when N >= 10 because digits are concatenated. Also, it does not output space-separated values as required, and for N=10, numbers like 10 cannot be represented as single digits in base-10 concatenation.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.5 , reason:  [This prints the permutation as a single integer, which fails when N >= 10 because digits are concatenated (e.g., 1 and 10 become 110), and even for N <= 9 it doesn't output space-separated values as required. Also, lexicographical order is not guaranteed due to numeric concatenation.]

17.reason: [Storing permutation as an integer by num*10+i breaks when any number in permutation is >=10 (e.g., N=10 includes '10'), since 10 cannot be represented as a single digit in base-10 concatenation. This leads to incorrect or malformed output.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.5 , reason:  [Storing permutation as an integer by num*10+i breaks when any number in permutation is >=10 (e.g., N=10 includes '10'), since 10 cannot be represented as a single digit in base-10 concatenation. This leads to incorrect or malformed output.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Storing permutation as an integer breaks for N >= 10 since multi-digit numbers cannot be represented by appending digits (e.g., 1 followed by 10 becomes 1*10 + 10 = 20, not "1 10").]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.5 , reason:  [Storing permutation as an integer breaks for N >= 10 since multi-digit numbers cannot be represented by single digits in base-10 concatenation. This causes incorrect output format and logic failure.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.5 , reason:  [Storing permutation as an integer breaks for N >= 10 since multi-digit numbers cannot be represented by single digits in base-10 concatenation. This causes incorrect output format and logic failure.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.5 , reason:  [Storing permutation as an integer by num*10+i breaks for N>=10 (since numbers have more than one digit) and also causes incorrect lexicographical ordering for N<=9 because permutations like [1,2] and [12] would be indistinguishable if N were larger; but more critically, the problem requires space-separated integers per line, not a single integer.]
